title: Commercial Roofing in Holt, MI | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Lansing
metaDescription: Commercial roofing for Holt's strip retail and light industrial roofs: repair-vs-replace math for smaller buildings, HVAC curb flashing failures, freeze-thaw risk.

html:
Holt's Roofs Are Smaller and the Repair-vs-Replace Math Is Tighter

Holt doesn't have the scale of an assembly plant or the regulatory weight of downtown Lansing. It's retail strips and light industrial parks with owner-operators who feel every dollar of a roofing budget, and the roofing decisions here are usually about stretching the life of what's already up there, not about which premium system to install. We approach Holt jobs with that in mind.

Strip Retail Roofs Built for a Different Era

A lot of the retail strips along Holt's commercial corridors were built decades ago for tenants that have since changed several times over, and the roof has usually seen at least one recover cycle since original construction. These are smaller roofs, often under 20,000 square feet, and that scale changes the economics of every decision. A full tear-off that would be routine on a big-box roof is a much bigger percentage hit to a small strip owner's budget, which is why we spend real time upfront figuring out whether a targeted repair or a partial recover actually solves the problem before recommending full replacement.

Multi-tenant strips also mean multiple HVAC units from different eras crowded onto a modest roof field, sometimes installed by whoever the individual tenant hired rather than a coordinated mechanical plan. We've found curbs on Holt strip roofs that were never properly sized for the unit sitting on them, just shimmed and caulked into place, and those are leak points waiting for the next freeze-thaw cycle to open them up.

Light Industrial Roofs, Smaller Scale Than Delta Township

The light industrial parks in Holt run a fraction of the size of the big manufacturing roofs elsewhere in the market, but they're not simple roofs just because they're smaller. Many carry process exhaust, compressor curbs, and loading dock canopies that need the same flashing attention a large plant roof needs, just over less square footage. Owner-operators running these buildings often handle roof maintenance themselves until something actually leaks, which means we're frequently called in after a small problem has already become a bigger one.

Repair Versus Replace: The Real Conversation in Holt

For a smaller commercial building, the repair-versus-replace decision has a lower threshold than it does for a large industrial roof, because the absolute dollar difference between the two options matters more relative to the owner's overall budget. We walk through this directly with Holt owners rather than defaulting to the bigger job:

  • How much of the existing membrane still has usable service life left in it
  • Whether the leak pattern is isolated to flashing details or spread across the field
  • What a targeted repair costs against what a full recover costs over a five-year horizon
  • Whether insurance or a warranty claim changes the math on either option
  • How much longer the building's current use is expected to continue

That last point matters more than owners sometimes expect. A building being held for a few more years before a planned sale or redevelopment doesn't need the same investment as one an owner plans to keep for another two decades, and we factor that into the recommendation rather than pushing the largest scope of work regardless of the owner's actual plans.

Freeze-Thaw on Small Roofs Isn't Small

A smaller roof doesn't mean smaller freeze-thaw risk. In some ways it's worse, because a strip retail roof with minimal slope and a handful of tightly packed HVAC curbs has fewer places for meltwater to go than a large open field roof does. We see ice damming form quickly around clustered rooftop units on Holt's smaller commercial buildings, and once that ice backs water up under a membrane lap, the leak often shows up inside the building well before spring.

Because these buildings are small enough that a single owner or a two-person maintenance team often handles everything, roofing problems here tend to get noticed later than on a property with a dedicated facilities staff. A stained ceiling tile might sit unreported for weeks in a strip retail unit before anyone connects it to a roof issue rather than a plumbing one. We encourage Holt owners to walk their own roofs after any major storm, even just a quick visual check, because catching a lifted membrane edge early is the difference between a same-day repair and a soaked insulation layer that has to be cut out.

Questions Holt Business Owners Ask Us

Is it worth replacing a roof on a building we might sell in a few years?

Not usually, if the current roof isn't actively failing. We look at your timeline honestly and often recommend targeted repair or a coating over full replacement when a sale is on the horizon.

Why does our strip mall roof leak near the HVAC units specifically?

Curbs installed by different mechanical contractors over the years aren't always properly sized or flashed for the unit sitting on them. That's the most common leak source we find on multi-tenant strip roofs in Holt.

Can a small industrial building's roof get a coating instead of a full replacement?

Often yes, if the membrane and deck are still sound. A silicone coating can add meaningful service life at a fraction of tear-off cost on a roof that's aging but not failing structurally.

How much does it cost to fix versus replace a strip retail roof?

It depends entirely on condition, but we always price both options side by side with a five-year cost comparison so the decision is based on numbers instead of which option sounds more thorough.

Why does a small roof still ice dam badly in winter?

Tightly clustered HVAC units on a low-slope roof create more obstacles for meltwater than an open field roof, so ice backs up faster even on a modest square footage.
